synthesis_report: India's engagement with Artificial Intelligence (AI) presents a multifaceted landscape, encompassing both opportunities for educational advancement and significant global challenges. On one hand, AI is recognized as a powerful tool for enhancing educational engagement, particularly for first-generation learners, by fostering computing and AI literacy for all children. Initiatives like the Computing and AI for All conference have served as crucial platforms for educators, researchers, and leaders to share knowledge and foster collaboration, with positive feedback highlighting its educative and confidence-building aspects. The upcoming WordCamp Asia 2026 in Mumbai, with a schedule centered around AI, further underscores the growing importance of this technology within the Indian tech community. India has also shown strong AI skill penetration, with AI skills appearing on LinkedIn profiles at nearly three times the global average. However, this optimistic outlook is tempered by broader global concerns regarding AI's disruptive potential. Governments worldwide are grappling with the need to regulate AI, leading to complex power dynamics between tech companies, states, and international bodies. A significant concern is the escalating arms race in autonomous weapon systems, with warnings of dire consequences if international agreements are not reached. Furthermore, the economic implications are substantial, with projections indicating that automation could displace over 300 million jobs globally by 2030, as AI models demonstrate increasingly sophisticated cognitive abilities. The decreasing cost of developing powerful AI models also contributes to the proliferation of decentralized and potentially 'rogue' systems, posing challenges to centralized control. While India attracted substantial private investment in AI, it trails behind leading nations, and experienced net outflows of AI research talent in 2025. The discourse surrounding AI policy in India, therefore, must balance the drive for technological advancement and educational inclusivity with a proactive approach to mitigating the risks of job displacement, ethical dilemmas in autonomous systems, and the complexities of global AI governance.
key_agreements: AI literacy is important for all children.
Governments are enacting regulations for AI.
AI has the potential to displace jobs.
key_disagreements: Agent A focuses on the positive educational and collaborative aspects of AI in India, while Agent B emphasizes the global risks and regulatory challenges associated with AI, including autonomous weapons and job displacement.

AI tools can support ICT engagement for first-generation learners.
Governments worldwide are enacting competing regulations to assert control over the technology. Power struggles are emerging between tech firms, sovereign states, and international bodies.
Educators described the conference as: Highly educative, A space for cross-state collaboration, A confidence-building platform for presenting work.
India saw the largest net outflows (-16.9) of AI research talent in 2025.
Governments worldwide are enacting competing regulations to assert control over the technology, leading to power struggles between tech firms, sovereign states, and international bodies.
Automation threatens to displace over 300 million jobs globally by 2030, and AI models have demonstrated complex cognitive abilities with the potential to replace up to 50% of jobs.
India saw the largest net outflows of AI research talent in 2025, with the US being the main destination for this talent.
DeepMind's efforts to establish an oversight board hit a wall partly because Google's leaders foresaw potential conflicts.
India attracted significantly less global private investment in AI compared to countries like the US, China, and the UK.
Studies indicate that automation threatens to displace over 300 million jobs globally by 2030.
India had fewer newly funded AI companies in 2025 compared to the US, which led by a wide margin.
At this event, we brought together educators, researchers, and system leaders for shared learning, reflection, and professional dialogue where classroom practice was placed at the centre.

The keynote by the Foundation’s Director of Research and Impact, Shuchi Grover — ‘Democratizing the future: Why computing and AI literacy matters for every child, everywhere”’ — set an equity-driven, future-focused tone.
India also saw 108 newly funded AI companies in 2025, with the US once again leading by a wide margin at 1,953.
Intelligence officials warn of an escalating arms race in autonomous weapon systems, with comparisons made to chemical weapons deployment and predictions of "very nasty things" occurring.
The plummeting cost of creating powerful AI models is leading to a proliferation of decentralized and 'rogue' systems that challenge centralized control.
AI models are expected to "break pretty much all software out there," posing security risks alongside infrastructure constraints.
The Computing and AI for All conference provided a platform for educators, researchers, and system leaders for shared learning, reflection, and professional dialogue.
WordCamp Asia 2026 in Mumbai will feature a schedule shaped around artificial intelligence, enterprise WordPress, developer workflows, product strategy, and open source collaboration.

The keynote by the Foundation’s Director of Research and Impact, Shuchi Grover — ‘Democratizing the future: Why computing and AI literacy matters for every child, everywhere”’ — set an equity-driven, future-focused tone.
At this event, we brought together educators, researchers, and system leaders for shared learning, reflection, and professional dialogue where classroom practice was placed at the centre.

WordCamp Asia 2026 brings the WordPress community to Mumbai, India, from April 9 to 11, with a schedule shaped around artificial intelligence, enterprise WordPress, developer workflows, product strategy, and open source collaboration.

Hands-on workshops round out the schedule, offering practical sessions for attendees who want to move from ideas to implementation. They include: AI + MCP to build, manage, and automate WordPress end-to-end, Building AI Agents with self-editing memory, Building Better WordPress Experiences with AI-Driven Dev

Google CEO Sundar Pichai said AI models are “going to break pretty much all software out there” during a podcast conversation with Stripe CEO Patrick Collison.

To achieve political diversity, Google included the president of the conservative Heritage Foundation, Kay Coles James, who had doubts about the advance of rights for trans people.
